reasoning: The question asks if there will be more than ten times as many fatalities in Taiwan for the 30 days before March 22, 2026, compared to one plus the 30-day average of fatalities over the 360 days preceding March 15, 2026. The baseline ACLED data states that as of March 6, 2026, the calculated baseline value is 1.000. This implies that there were exactly zero ACLED-recorded fatalities over the prior 360 days. Thus, the target threshold for the 30-day period (from Feb 20 to Mar 22) is to have > 10 * 1.00 = 10 fatalities (so 11 or more fatalities). We are predicting on March 15, meaning 23 of the 30 days have already elapsed. Web searches for violent events, military clashes, protests, or riots in Taiwan in early 2026 reveal no mass-casualty events matching the ACLED inclusion criteria (battles, explosions, organized violence against civilians, fatal riots). A December 2025 stabbing was evidently not coded as an ACLED incident (lone wolf crime rather than organized political violence/riot), or if it was, it somehow rounded down to an average that still gave 1.000 (which is mathematically possible if 4 deaths over 360 days averages to 4/12 = ~0.33 per month, so 1.333, but the prompt explicitly states 1.000, meaning 0 ACLED deaths). Since the target is 11 or more ACLED fatalities in a highly peaceful Taiwan, with only 7 days remaining and no current ongoing conflict or riot, the probability is extremely low. I predict the lowest allowable probability of 0.02.
